video_title: Stocks Slide as Wall Street Gets AI Wake-up Call | The Close 06/23/2026
gist: Bloomberg 'The Close' on the AI-selloff day, with distinct interviews and earnings. ProShares' de Longis calls it a normal pullback (watch credit spreads, not price, for real contagion) and stays overweight equities funded from rich credit. Goldman equities trading nears a $5B+ record on volatility/Asia; JPM's Chad Tredway shows AI is DRIVING office/housing demand (SF best absorption in 30 years). FedEx beats post-freight-spinoff on pricing power (+14% revenue on +2% volume); KB Home rises on guidance with 'spring-loaded' incentive-driven margin upside; Cerebras' wafer-scale inference leans on OpenAI (circular revenue). Plus Permanent Portfolio's contrarian Meta/software longs, a Morgan Stanley private-credit redemption cap, and Meta entering prediction markets (Robinhood/DraftKings dip).

themes.theme: ProShares: a normal pullback, watch credit spreads

insights.claim: ProShares CIO Alessio de Longis calls the selloff a normal pullback of an earnings-driven momentum trade — ~30% S&P 500 earnings growth (most since 2021) plus a +50bps move on the long end made the repricing of long-duration tech rational, not a top.
insights.speaker: Alessio de Longis (ProShares CIO)
insights.so_what: Frames the drop as healthy repricing against higher yields — supportive of staying overweight equities.

insights.claim: His strategy: overweight equities funded FROM credit markets (spreads at all-time lows offer no upside) with a barbell extending government-bond duration (50bps of Fed hikes priced by March 2027), and broaden into value mid/small caps given extreme concentration.
insights.speaker: Alessio de Longis
insights.so_what: A concrete allocation: fund equity overweight from rich credit, hedge with duration, and diversify out of mega-cap concentration.

insights.claim: de Longis's tell for distinguishing a technical pullback from real contagion is whether CREDIT SPREADS widen — spreads are the mechanism that amplifies leverage and contagion — and they remain remarkably resilient, signaling just a technical selloff (an energy spike would void the whole long-duration thesis).
insights.speaker: Alessio de Longis
insights.so_what: Watch credit spreads, not price action, as the real contagion gauge for the AI/leverage trade.
insights.quote: ARE WE SEEING CREDIT SPREADS BEGIN TO WIDEN?

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs equities trading is on track for ~$5B+ in Q2 (near Q1's $5.3B record), driven by volatility, SpaceX activity and a booming Asia (Korea +100% YTD) — what was a FULL YEAR of equities revenue a decade ago — while the AI-infrastructure trade grows increasingly crowded among hedge funds.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Natarajan (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Trading-desk records are a read on volatility/Asia flows; crowded AI-infra positioning is the latent risk if dispersion hits.
insights.quote: ABOUT 10 YEARS AGO THAT WAS THE FULL YEAR EQUITIES TRADING REVENUE

insights.claim: JPM Asset Management's Chad Tredway says — contrary to 'AI takes jobs' — AI is DRIVING office demand: San Francisco posted its best absorption quarter in 30 years on AI tenants, with Bay Area rents up ~10% and AI-wealth fueling housing demand.
insights.speaker: Chad Tredway (JPM Asset Mgmt)
insights.so_what: AI is a concrete office/housing tailwind in tech hubs, not just a labor threat — a contrarian real-estate angle.
insights.quote: WE SEE OFFICE DEMAND BEING DRIVEN BY AI

insights.claim: Tredway plays the data-center SUPPLY CHAIN (manufacturing tenants making chips/wafers, housing around tech hubs) rather than data centers directly, using JPM data on half the US population to call the Sunbelt: current softness but no new supply pipeline implies higher rent growth into 2027.
insights.speaker: Chad Tredway
insights.so_what: An indirect way to play AI via real assets — supply-chain real estate plus Sunbelt absorption as new supply dries up.

insights.claim: FedEx beat post-freight-spinoff (Express revenue +14% on just +2% volume = pricing power, op margin 8.4%, EPS $6.31), guiding CY2026 to $16.90-18.10; Citi ($287 PT) frames the bull case around an unprecedented $6B free-cash-flow target supporting a >$100B market cap, with pricing discipline and DRIVE network integration (done by 2027) the keys — though the stock slipped on high expectations (+80%/yr).
insights.speaker: Ariel Rosa (Citi) / anchors
insights.so_what: The revenue-vs-volume gap confirms pricing power as the FedEx thesis; operating leverage explodes if volume ever turns.
insights.quote: $6 BILLION FREE CASH FLOW TARGET, A NUMBER WE HAVE NEVER SEEN FROM FEDEX

insights.claim: KB Home's results fell sharply (revenue -27%, deliveries -23%, EPS $0.43 vs $1.50) yet the stock rose on above-consensus guidance; UBS sees 2026 as a better housing year (intent-to-buy 36% vs 30% avg, rates -25bps YoY) and calls builders 'spring-loaded' — every 25bps of incentive pullback adds ~100bps of margin, with stocks at ~11x 2027 earnings.
insights.speaker: John Lovallo (UBS) / anchors
insights.so_what: Homebuilder margin optionality is large if elevated (9-10%) incentives roll back — the key swing for the group.
insights.quote: EVERY 25 BASIS POINTS IS 100 BASIS POINTS OF MARGIN

insights.claim: Cerebras (first earnings since its May IPO) makes an iPad-sized wafer-scale chip for ultra-fast single-query inference — a TSMC-exclusive capability behind its OpenAI and AWS deals — with cloud revenue beating at $82.8M and Q2 guidance $194M vs $173M est, though the stock dipped; an analyst flags 'circular revenue' (OpenAI) and that Nvidia's flexibility plus Groq's LPUs make Cerebras a niche play.
insights.speaker: analyst / anchors
insights.so_what: Cerebras is a concentrated fast-inference bet leaning on OpenAI — high specialization but circular-revenue and Nvidia-competition caveats.

insights.claim: Permanent Portfolio's manager warns 'safety is a dangerous word' and that a less-communicative, inflation-fighting Warsh Fed could make markets more volatile and multidirectional — not reflexively buy-the-dip — favoring diversification and skepticism over one-directional bets.
insights.speaker: Michael (Permanent Portfolio)
insights.so_what: Less Fed hand-holding raises the odds that 'buy every dip' stops working — argues for genuine diversification.
insights.quote: SAFETY IS A DANGEROUS WORD. THERE IS NO SAFETY

insights.claim: He flags contrarian longs: Meta at ~18x is a below-market multiple (attractive despite AI-capex risk), and software — sold off on the 'AI will ruin software' theory he rejects — is a long-term opportunity for patient investors.
insights.speaker: Michael (Permanent Portfolio)
insights.so_what: Two specific contrarian longs against the prevailing AI-capex and AI-kills-software narratives.
insights.quote: SOFTWARE GENERALLY SPEAKING HAS BEEN SOLD OFF ON THE THEORY THAT AI WILL RUIN SOFTWARE. WE NEVER BOUGHT THAT STORY

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley capped redemptions at 5% on a North Haven private-income fund that received a 12% redemption request — a private-credit liquidity-stress signal.
insights.speaker: anchors
insights.so_what: Redemption gating in private credit is an early liquidity-warning to watch as the asset class scales.
insights.quote: MORGAN STANLEY HAS CAP REDEMPTIONS ON ANOTHER FUND AT 5%

insights.claim: Robinhood and DraftKings fell after an NYT report that Mark Zuckerberg/Meta is building a Polymarket-style prediction-market app — a competitive threat to the prediction/betting space; separately IBM rose 5% on a JPM upgrade ($291 PT) and joining OpenAI's cyber partner program, plus quantum momentum (IBM targets quantum-advantage proof by end-2026).
insights.speaker: anchors
insights.so_what: Meta entering prediction markets is a new competitive overhang for Robinhood/DraftKings; IBM gets an AI-cyber + quantum catalyst.

insights.claim: Carnival fell ~5% on weak Q3 net-yield guidance (pricing/discounting plus a Middle East drag), though anchors note the cruise consumer is famously loyal and sticky; the NYC World Cup is forecast at 1.2M visitors and ~$3B impact with international visitors ~50% of spend and hotels ~90% occupancy.
insights.speaker: anchors / Julie Coker
insights.so_what: A consumer read: cruise pricing softness vs a World Cup tourism bump (partially offset by Iran/dollar-driven cuts to NYC's full-year tourism forecast).
insights.quote: WE WERE FORECASTING 1.2 MILLION VISITORS FOR THE REGION
